author: @garybrown2039
text:
Hello, Robert. I just wanted to leave you this comment because I have a question. I happened to be watching a few of your videos about Sterling engines. And I am in the need of one for a personal heat experiment that I'm doing out of fun.

In general, I'm looking for a Sterling engine that can produce a reasonable amount of torque for a shaft (comparible to the strength of human fingers trying to move sone magnets on a shaft) once I have it on a hot service.

That surface will be roughly 10 to 11 inches in diameter and hot as 400 Degree F. 

I see a lot of promise in gamma type Sterling engines, especially that magnetic one that you had a short video on. 

By any chance, can you recommend a very simple but effective design for someone like me who is new to this to try out. 

I would prefer to have diagrams to show what parts i need and how to fit them in. I tried looking at that NASA design that you did a video on, but I just can't see enough of it directly. And I can't find the original file.


P.S. Thank you for reading this and for your channel. Overall, it has been a great source of inspiration for me who wants to get into this type of hobby.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate - do you mean buy one or make one?

replies.author: @garybrown2039
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  Either will do. However I will prefer to make one eventually.

author: @davidconner-shover51
text:
Ever since you put out the crossed roller bearing idea, I've been using them extensively on my load bearing, but not high speed build projects.
My current project is a solar panel tracking mount involving a 12 inch (300mm) lazy susan at the base, with two 1 1/2" (38mm) shaft bearings. The cheapest shaft bearings I could find were 7 times the material cost of making them purely out of ABS-GF ($7 vs $70, and without the outdoor environmental stability) and lithium grease. Since they are not in a high speed application, I don't expect them to wear out in the expected 10-15 year operational life. if not, they are fairly cheap to replace. I also don't have to worry much about the bearings rusting out

author: @GavinImpett
text: If that was made out of metal, It would cost several thousand dollars. I doubt airsoft pellets would hold up, for a variety of reason. I think you are on the right track with nylon.  Boxing printer two and setting up printer tomorrow, hopefully it is the charm.  I was hoping you would come up with this design, a bearing with a big hole in the middle.

replies.author: @RobotWorker100percentLoyal
replies.text: 6mm airsoft BBs, except glass instead of plastic?

replies.author: @GavinImpett
replies.text: @RobotWorker100percentLoyal the main problems with airsoft is they are built to compress on impact, so in that environment they would probably squish, jam, and melt, glass or not. My thinking, which I think shadows Rob's, with the exception of wires and magnets, the wind turbine is best served as entirely 3d printable. Rollers solves that problem without getting into machined balls. A roller is good enough. A lot of people don't have access to an electrical grid. A lot of people who never had access to a land-line telephone have cell phones.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: I like this idea. Would be interesting to see if you get any kind of real efficiency boost, or does the increased surface contact result in more load...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it should give a boost mate

replies.author: @66moonraker
replies.text: Multi hundreds of tonnes loads are moved on "ordinary" roads simply because the point loading is reduced by increasing the number of wheels (or rollers) to distribute the gross weight of the vehicle over an increased number of points of contact. "Robert's" bearing produces a similar outcome. The gross weight remains the same, but the point loading on each roller is reduced and all the more so if the bearing circumference is greater (so more rollers). The resultant reduction of weight imposed on each roller should mean less friction and therefore less wear. That's the theory of an old worn out retired truck driver, so what do I know?

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: @ Great points... real world friction will be less due to less deformation! Thank you for the insights.
